feedback.text: When it comes to government intervention in the digital market we are touching an especially sensitive topic for me. That's because Im someone who firmly believes that the 21st article of the Italian constitution (freedom of expression) should never be crossed under any circumstances. Under the protection of this article there is any form of media including videogames. However, the previous years have demonstrated just how easily companies can get away with manipulative designs to pressure people into making impulsive purchases, particularly in the form of microtransactions. The Digital Fairness Act seems to address many of the most pressing issues in this area. Its relevance is even clearer when considered alongside the recent EU investigation into the game Star Stable, which highlighted several of these harmful practices. But Star Stable is just the tip of the iceberg. The same exploitative strategies are used across the industry, from lesser-known titles like Overmortal to global giants like League of Legends. While the act rightly emphasizes the protection of children (which is absolutely necessary), its important to recognize that these techniques can be even more effective on adults. Unlike minors, adults dont need to ask anyone for permission before spending money. That makes them even more vulnerable to games specifically designed to manipulate behavior using dark patterns. These games often build their entire monetization strategy around such techniques: introducing multiple premium currencies, using time-limited events to create pressure, or, in some cases, designing the gameplay itself to be pay-to-win. The goal is often to turn a portion of the player base into whales (people who spend large sums of money, often because they struggle with impulse control or gambling-like behavior). That alone should qualify them as vulnerable individuals deserving of protection. To make matters worse, many of these games are entirely server-dependent. At any moment, the company can shut down the servers and leave players with nothing. This is an issue that deserves either inclusion in the Digital Fairness Act or follow-up through the Stop Destroying Games initiative. For all these reasons, I believe it's time for the EU to take meaningful action. Whether minors or adults, EU citizens deserve protection from companies that design games to exploit rather than entertain.
